Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. At 28, I've got a lot going on outside of uni, so I'd usually squeeze in study sessions whenever I could find a gap. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often try to work through practical examples rather than just reading. Most weeks I'd get maybe 8 hours in total, spread across random evenings and weekends when I felt like I could focus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly cram when deadlines loomed. With 15 courses to juggle, I couldn't really stick to a rigid schedule even if I wanted to. Life just got in the way too often for consistency.
Q3: Usually it was an assignment deadline or a notification that pushed me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about something due and think 'oh right, better check that.' I wasn't one of those students who logged in just to browse around or stay on top of things proactively. It was more reactive than anything.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd use it when I absolutely had to, but it wasn't something I thought about naturally. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped a bit—made it feel less like a chore—but I still wouldn't say it became routine.
Q5: The video content was probably the most useful for me because I could watch someone actually doing things rather than just reading about theory. I watched quite a bit of video content when I did engage. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful too, seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives on the material.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional resources. If it wasn't directly tied to an assessment, I probably skipped it. I just didn't have the bandwidth to explore extras when I was already struggling to keep up with the core requirements across all my courses.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was stuck on. The gamified elements actually made some tasks feel more worthwhile—there was something satisfying about completing challenges and seeing progress. But if something felt like busywork, I'd mentally check out.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required stuff had this weight to it—I knew I had to do it eventually. Optional stuff? I'd look at it and think 'that's nice, but I've got bigger priorities.' The anxiety of falling behind on required work always won out over exploring optional content.
Q9: Absolutely. Once I downloaded lecture slides or readings, I'd often study from those offline. I'd print things out sometimes, or work through problems on paper away from my computer. Being a kinesthetic learner, I actually retained more when I was physically writing things out or manipulating materials rather than clicking around online.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be at my desk working through practice problems on paper, or discussing concepts with a mate over coffee, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd sometimes re-watch downloaded videos on my tablet during commutes. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study time.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a video, look at it once online, then spend hours with it offline. My LMS activity would show one quick access, but I might have spent an entire evening working through that material. It was just more convenient to work offline without needing to stay logged in.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. The anxiety really ramped up when multiple assignments converged, and I'd sometimes freeze up instead of being productive. There were weeks where I felt overwhelmed by everything and just... disengaged mentally. The stress made it hard to even start tasks, which only made the anxiety worse.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Competing responsibilities meant I'd sometimes go days without logging in. Confusion about assignment requirements also made me avoid the platform—if I didn't understand what was expected, I'd procrastinate rather than seek clarification.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was elsewhere. I'd have the LMS open, technically 'studying,' but I was really just going through the motions. The information wasn't sticking because I was too stressed or distracted to properly engage with it.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I wasn't sure how to approach them. The anxiety would build up around certain tasks, and avoiding them felt easier in the moment even though I knew it would make things worse later. It's a frustrating cycle I've struggled with.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated everything. My LMS usage would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd suddenly become very active, cramming in all the content I should have engaged with earlier. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have logged in much at all.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always in predictable ways. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar content. But if feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, I'd sometimes avoid that module for a while out of frustration or embarrassment. The gamified feedback was easier to handle somehow.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag. Having clear deadlines helped me prioritize, but the sheer volume of 15 courses meant I was always triaging. Modules that were well-organized and had clear expectations were easier to engage with. Vague or disorganized sections made me more likely to disengage.
Q19: The gamified tasks genuinely helped make engagement easier—they broke things into manageable chunks and gave me a sense of progress. But some aspects made it harder, like when there was too much content without clear guidance on what was essential. I needed structure because I wasn't going to create it myself.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand requirements without actually doing the work yet. Or they could be like me—accessing things with good intentions but getting distracted or overwhelmed before completing anything. High activity doesn't necessarily mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. I performed decently—got a Distinction—despite not being super active on the LMS. I learned a lot offline, used my own methods, and focused my limited online time on what directly impacted my grades. The LMS activity doesn't capture how I actually learned.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns and deadline-driven behavior. They capture when students access content and submit work. But they completely miss offline study, the quality of engagement, emotional state, or whether someone actually understood what they were looking at. It's quantity over quality.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and thinking critically, not just going through the motions. For me, real engagement happens when I'm hands-on with something, working through problems, making connections—regardless of whether I'm logged into any system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much that affected my engagement patterns. It wouldn't capture the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, or the times I was genuinely interested in a topic but explored it through external resources. It also misses that I'm actually happy with how things turned out despite appearing disengaged online.
